comments.text: Some progress on this problem and its $B_r$ extension. The main constructions are vibe formalized in Lean 4/Mathlib — zero sorries, standard axioms, the audit prints at build time — here. 1. The $B_r$ extension (in its nontrivial reading — some $\varepsilon(r,k)>0$): resolved affirmatively for all $r,k\ge2$, with any $\varepsilon<\frac{r+1}{r^2}$. For odd $r$: $n=(t^r-1)^r$, so $B_r(n)=n$ and $n+1=t^r\Psi_r(t)$; Schur+Hensel force $s^r\mid\Psi_r(t)$ with $s^r\asymp t$ by taking $t$ in one period. Even $r$: same with $n=(t^r+1)^r-1$. 2. The $k=3$ lower bound strengthens to $\limsup_n \frac{B_2(n)B_2(n+1)B_2(n+2)}{n^2\log n}=\infty$: run the Pell construction with a finite set $S$ of primes $\equiv5\pmod 8$ simultaneously ($\alpha^{(p+1)/2\cdot p}\equiv-1\bmod p^2$, odd quotients), giving $B_2(n_j+2)\ge\prod_{p\in S}p^2$ with $\log n_j\ll\prod_{p\in S}\frac{p+1}2p$; the gain is $\ge\prod_{p\in S}\frac{2p}{p+1}\to\infty$. 3. For two-term cube-full parts, $E_3:=\limsup_n\frac{\log(B_3(n)B_3(n+1))}{\log n}$ satisfies\[ \tfrac{40}{27}\ \le\ E_3\ \le\ \tfrac32, \]the upper bound conditional on $abc$, the lower bound via an explicit degree-27 Davenport–Zannier identity $G^3+N^3=HC^3$ ($N=2^{14}3^4$, $G'=9C^2$) plus the same Hensel device; a rational identity of this shape of degree $3d$ gives $E_3\ge\frac32-\frac1{6d}$, so a family with $d\to\infty$ would pin $E_3=\frac32$ under $abc$. Also recorded: under $abc$, $\prod_{n\le m<n+k}B_2(m)\ll_{k,\varepsilon}n^{2+\varepsilon}$ for every fixed $k$ (Granville–Langevin radical bound applied to $\prod_{i<k}(x+i)$). The unconditional weak form of course remains open.

If $k \le 2$, then $$\prod_{n \le m < n+k} B_2(m) \le n(n+1) \le 2n^2, \hspace{50pt} (1)$$so the supposed upper bounds of $n^{2+o(1)}$ and $c_kn^2$ are non-trivial only for $k \ge 3$. But already for $k = 3$ I believe that for every $c$, the left-hand side of $(1)$ is larger than $cn^2$ infinitely often. Let $(x_j, y_j)$ be the solutions to the Pell equation $x^2 - 8y^2 = 1$, with $(x_0, y_0) = (1, 0)$ and $(x_{j+1}, y_{j+1}) = (3x_j + 8y_j, x_j + 3y_j)$. Taking $n_j = 8y_j^2$, we note $B_2(n_j) = n_j$ and $B_2(n_j+1) = n_j+1$. Now define the indices $j_1 = 1$ and, for $t \ge 1$, $j_{t+1} = 5j_t + 2$. Then I think that the congruence relation $$x_{j_t}^2 \equiv -1 \pmod{5^t} \hspace{116pt} (2)$$holds for all $t$, and I am sure someone here is able to verify that $(2)$ (or something very similar) does indeed hold. Assuming $(2)$ for the moment, we have $x_j = e^{O(j)}$ and $j_t = O(5^t)$, so that $n_{j_t} < x_{j_t}^2 = e^{O(5^t)}$. We deduce that $B_2(n_{j_t}+2) \ge 5^t > c \log n_{j_t}$ for some positive $c$ and all $t \ge 2$. For all $k \ge 3$ we therefore obtain $$\prod_{n \le m < n+k} B_2(m) \ge \prod_{n \le m < n+3} B_2(m) > c n^2 \log n$$infinitely often. And I would guess that this lower bound is probably improvable. On the final question concerning $B_r(m)$ for $r \ge 3$, am I correct in thinking that it asks for some $\epsilon = \epsilon(r, k) > 0$ for which the $\limsup$ is unbounded? If so, then the existence of a positive $\epsilon(r, k)$ trivially implies the existence of a positive $\epsilon(r', k')$ for all pairs $(r', k')$ with $r' \le r$ and $k' \ge k$. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Gemini Deepthink confirms that your argument negatively answers the second version of the problem. One can streamline the argument as follows: 1. Define $n_j = (\alpha^{2j} - 2 + \alpha^{-2j})/4$, where $\alpha = 3 + \sqrt{8}$ (so $\alpha^{-1} = 3 - \sqrt{8}$). Then \begin{align*} n_j &= 8 \times \left[\frac{\alpha^j - \alpha^{-j}}{2\sqrt{8}}\right]^2 \\ n_j+1 &= \left[\frac{\alpha^j + \alpha^{-j}}{2}\right]^2 \\ n_j+2 &= \frac{\alpha^{2j} + 6 + \alpha^{-2j}}{4} \end{align*} and both expressions in brackets are integers (OEIS A001109 and A001541 respectively). In particular, $n_j$ is a natural number (OEIS A132592), with $n_j, n_j+1$ already $2$-full. 2. Observe the identity $\alpha^{\pm 3} = 99 \pm 35 \sqrt{8} = - 1 + 5 \times (20 \pm 7 \sqrt{8})$. By induction one can show that $\alpha^{\pm 3 \times 5^{t-1}} = -1 + 5^t (a_t \pm b_t \sqrt{8})$ for various integers $a_t,b_t$ and all $t \geq 1$. Now we set $j_t :=(3 \times 5^{t-1}-1)/2$ (which is an integer) and compute \begin{align*} 4(n_{j_t}+2) &= \alpha^{-1} \alpha^{3 \times 5^{t-1}} + 6 + \alpha \alpha^{-3 \times 5^{t-1}} \\ &= (3-\sqrt{8}) (-1 + 5^t (a_t + b_t \sqrt{8})) + 6 + (3+\sqrt{8}) (-1 + 5^t (a_t - b_t \sqrt{8}))\\ &= 5^t (6 a_t - 16 b_t) \end{align*} giving the key claim $5^t | n_{j_t}+2$. Thus for $t \geq 2$ $$ \prod_{n_{j_t} \leq m < n_{j_t}+3} B_2(m) \geq n_{j_t} (n_{j_t}+1) 5^t \gg n_{j_t}^2 \log n_{j_t}.$$ This looks within range of being "vibe formalizable" in Lean, if anyone wants to give it a shot.
Nice, thanks for verifying Terence! And apologies that I let myself get distracted with other problems, instead of focusing on more important stuff ;)

I have formalized this result in Lean. I used Aristotle from Harmonic, specifically its recently-added functionality of auto-formalizing directly from English/LaTeX source. I wrote the actual final theorem statement somewhat by hand in order to be more confident that it's reasonable. Type-check it online!

How to use Aristotle? When I press "Get access" on their website, I see message "We'll send an exclusive invitation to this email as soon as it's your turn.", and then I waited several days but still no e-mail from them. I am not even sure that my request is recorded and I am indeed in the queue.

For completeness, I also asked ChatGPT DeepResearch and Gemini DeepResearch to do literature reviews. Both tools identified the problem as #367, though they also got slightly confused by also considering the closely related problem #366. Both AIs noted the Pell equation construction that is relevant to both problems, and mentioned some further work on consecutive powerful numbers (particularly assuming the abc conjecture) of relevance more to #366 to #367, but did not detect the disproof of the second part of this problem that had already been made in the comments, and both declared the problem to be open.
